title: Electrical Fundamentals for Field Use
summary: Ohm's Law, power formulas, series vs parallel circuits, wire gauge selection, multimeter usage, and electrical safety for field applications
body:
## Electrical Fundamentals for Field Use

Understanding basic electricity lets you diagnose problems, size components, and make safe repairs. This is not academic theory — every formula here has direct practical application in the field.

## The Three Fundamentals

### Voltage (V) — Electrical Pressure

- Measured in volts (V)
- Think of it as water pressure in a pipe
- Common voltages: 1.5V (AA battery), 12V (car battery), 120V (US household), 240V (US heavy appliances), 230V (European household)

### Current (I) — Electrical Flow

- Measured in amps (A)
- Think of it as the amount of water flowing through the pipe
- A 60W light bulb draws 0.5 amps at 120V
- A car starter motor draws 100 to 200 amps at 12V

### Resistance (R) — Opposition to Flow

- Measured in ohms (the Greek letter omega)
- Think of it as the pipe diameter — narrow pipes resist flow
- A wire has low resistance. An insulator has very high resistance.
- Corroded connections, undersized wire, and poor joints all increase resistance.

## Ohm's Law — The Master Formula

**V = I x R** (Voltage = Current x Resistance)

Rearranged:
- **I = V / R** (Current = Voltage / Resistance)
- **R = V / I** (Resistance = Voltage / Current)

### Practical Examples

- A 12V circuit with a 6-ohm load draws 2 amps (12 / 6 = 2)
- You need a resistor to limit current to 0.5A in a 12V circuit: R = 12 / 0.5 = 24 ohms
- A wire reads 0.5 ohms when it should read near 0: you have a bad connection adding resistance

## Power Formula

**P = V x I** (Power in watts = Voltage x Current)

Rearranged:
- **I = P / V** (Current = Power / Voltage)
- **V = P / I**

### Practical Examples

- A 1500W space heater on 120V draws 12.5 amps (1500 / 120 = 12.5). This needs a 15A or 20A circuit.
- A 100W 12V device draws 8.3 amps. Size wire and fuse accordingly.
- To find how much power a solar panel can deliver to a 12V battery: a 100W panel at peak produces about 8.3 amps.

## Series vs Parallel Circuits

### Series Circuit

Components connected end-to-end in a single path.

- **Current** is the SAME through all components
- **Voltage** DIVIDES across components (V_total = V1 + V2 + V3)
- **Resistance** ADDS (R_total = R1 + R2 + R3)
- If any component fails open, the entire circuit stops working
- Example: Old-style Christmas lights — one bulb burns out, entire string goes dark

**When to use series:**
- Batteries: connect in series to increase voltage (four 1.5V AA batteries in series = 6V)
- Solar panels: connect in series to increase voltage for a charge controller
- Voltage dividers

### Parallel Circuit

Components connected across the same two points.

- **Voltage** is the SAME across all components
- **Current** DIVIDES among paths (I_total = I1 + I2 + I3)
- **Resistance** decreases (1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3)
- If one component fails, the others keep working
- Example: Household wiring — each outlet is in parallel. Unplugging a lamp does not affect other outlets.

**When to use parallel:**
- Batteries: connect in parallel to increase capacity (amp-hours) while maintaining the same voltage
- Solar panels: connect in parallel to increase current
- Multiple loads that need to operate independently

## Wire Gauge Guide (AWG — American Wire Gauge)

Smaller AWG number = thicker wire = more current capacity.

| AWG | Diameter | Max Amps (Chassis) | Common Use |
|-----|----------|-------------------|------------|
| 22 | 0.64mm | 5A | Sensors, low-power signals |
| 18 | 1.02mm | 10A | LED lights, small accessories |
| 16 | 1.29mm | 15A | Radios, instruments |
| 14 | 1.63mm | 20A | 15A household circuits |
| 12 | 2.05mm | 25A | 20A household circuits |
| 10 | 2.59mm | 35A | 30A circuits, dryers, AC |
| 8 | 3.26mm | 50A | Sub-panels, ranges |
| 6 | 4.11mm | 65A | Sub-panels, large motors |
| 4 | 5.19mm | 85A | Service entrance |
| 2 | 6.54mm | 115A | Battery cables |

**Long runs require upsizing.** For every 50 feet of wire run (round trip), go up one gauge to compensate for voltage drop.

## Multimeter Usage

A multimeter is the single most useful electrical diagnostic tool.

### DC Voltage (V with straight line)

- Set dial to DC voltage range appropriate for your circuit (20V for car, 200V or 750V for household)
- Red probe to positive, black probe to negative/ground
- Read the voltage directly
- Use this to test batteries, alternator output, voltage drops across connections

### AC Voltage (V with wavy line)

- **CAUTION: Household AC voltage (120V/240V) can kill.**
- Set dial to AC voltage, 200V or 750V range
- Touch probes to hot and neutral (or hot and ground)
- Should read approximately 120V for US outlets

### Resistance (Omega symbol)

- **Circuit must be de-energized** (power off, component disconnected)
- Set dial to the expected resistance range
- Touch probes to both ends of the component
- Good wire reads near 0 ohms. A broken wire reads OL (open line / infinite resistance).
- Use this to test continuity of wires, heating elements, and coils

### Continuity (Speaker/Diode symbol)

- The most useful setting for field diagnosis
- Beeps when there is a low-resistance path between the probes
- Use this to trace wires, find breaks, test switches, and check fuses
- **Always test with the circuit de-energized**

### Current (A)

- **The meter must be placed IN SERIES with the circuit** (break the circuit and insert the meter)
- This is the one measurement where incorrect setup can blow the meter fuse or damage the meter
- Set to the appropriate range (usually 10A maximum for most meters)
- Use the dedicated current jacks on the meter (usually a separate red jack marked 10A)

## Fuse and Breaker Sizing

**The fuse protects the WIRE, not the device.** Size the fuse based on the wire gauge:

- 22 AWG wire: 5A fuse
- 18 AWG wire: 10A fuse
- 16 AWG wire: 15A fuse
- 14 AWG wire: 15A fuse (15A breaker for household)
- 12 AWG wire: 20A fuse (20A breaker)
- 10 AWG wire: 30A fuse (30A breaker)

**Never replace a fuse with a higher rated one.** The wire will overheat before the fuse blows, starting a fire.

## Safety

- **Respect voltage:** 50V DC or 30V AC can be lethal under the right conditions
- Always assume a wire is live until proven otherwise
- Test with a multimeter before touching
- Never work on household wiring with the circuit energized
- Use one hand when probing live circuits (keeps current from crossing your heart)
- Wear rubber-soled shoes on damp ground
- Water and electricity do not mix — never work on electrical in wet conditions
- Keep a fire extinguisher rated for electrical fires (Class C / CO2) nearby
